title: The Michelson interferometer: an instrument built to find the ether, repurposed to hear black holes collide
intro: Albert A. Michelson designed his interferometer in 1881 to settle one of the great open questions of nineteenth-century physics: through what medium did light propagate, and what was Earth's motion through it. The experiment, repeated with Edward Morley in 1887 to a precision that should have been overkill, returned the null result that broke the ether hypothesis and helped force the special theory of relativity into existence twenty-eight years later. The instrument itself, with a beam splitter that divides a coherent wave into two arms, a mirror at the end of each arm that reflects each piece back, and a recombiner where the two pieces interfere, turned out to be the most sensitive length-comparator humans had ever invented, and by 2015 a kilometer-scale version of it was reading the displacement of a freely-falling mirror to one part in 10²¹. The same instrument failed to find the ether and succeeded at hearing two black holes merge.

sections.heading: 1. Why Michelson built it: the ether, and Earth's wind through it

blocks.text: By the 1860s the wave theory of light, established a century earlier by Young and Fresnel against Newton's corpuscular opposition, had hardened into a problem. Every other known wave (sound in air, ripples on a pond, vibrations in a string) needed a material to wave in. Light, evidently, also waved (the interference fringes of Young's double slit and the rainbows out of Fresnel's prism settled that). What was the medium? The community had named it the luminiferous ether: a perfectly transparent, weightless substance filling the whole universe, sufficiently rigid to support the very fast transverse oscillations Maxwell's 1865 equations demanded (c ≈ 3 × 10⁸ m/s), yet so tenuous that planets passed through it without any measurable drag. The ether was the silent assumption inside every wave-optics calculation of the period, and nobody had ever detected it.

Maxwell, the year before he died, pointed out in an 1879 letter to D. P. Todd at the Nautical Almanac Office that the situation might be testable. If the ether was at rest (or near rest) in some absolute frame, then Earth's orbital velocity around the Sun, about 30 km/s, should drag light against an apparent ether wind. The two-way travel time of a beam sent along the direction of Earth's motion and back would differ from the two-way travel time of a beam sent across it. The fractional effect was tiny, of order (v/c)² ≈ 10⁻⁸, which Maxwell judged below the reach of any astronomical method then available. The remark was published posthumously in Nature in 1880, where it was read by a young naval officer named Albert Michelson, who decided that a laboratory measurement could in principle reach 10⁻⁸ if one used interference of light to compare the two travel times directly.

Michelson's insight was that the absolute travel times need not be measured at all; only their difference, expressed in wavelengths of the source light. With sodium light at λ ≈ 589 nm (the bright yellow doublet of the sodium D lines, then a standard laboratory source), a path-length difference of one wavelength shifts the interference pattern at the recombiner by exactly one fringe. The 10⁻⁸ effect Maxwell wanted, on a one-meter arm, corresponds to ten nanometers of optical path difference, which at 589 nm is a fringe shift of about 0.03. Reading fringe positions to better than 0.01 of a fringe by eye is routine. The problem became a matter of building a stiff enough apparatus and steering enough light through it, and Michelson built the first instrument at the Helmholtz laboratory in Potsdam in 1881.

sections.heading: 2. The geometry: a beam splitter, two arms, a recombiner

blocks.text: The instrument has four optical elements. A collimated source emits a quasi-monochromatic plane wave of wavelength λ. A beam splitter (a thin glass plate with a partially reflecting coating on one side, oriented at 45° to the incoming beam) divides the incoming wave into two daughter waves of roughly equal amplitude: one transmitted straight through to one arm, the other reflected sideways at 90° into a perpendicular arm. A mirror at the end of each arm (call them M₁ at distance L₁ from the splitter and M₂ at distance L₂ from the splitter) sends each daughter wave back along its own arm. The two returning daughter waves meet again at the beam splitter, where each is again split and recombined; the half that emerges along the original output direction is the recombined wave the detector records. Walk through the geometry and the result is that each output photon has travelled a round trip of 2L₁ along arm 1, or a round trip of 2L₂ along arm 2.

The recombined wave is the sum of two equal-amplitude pieces that have travelled different distances. Their path difference is Δd = 2L₂ − 2L₁ = 2(L₂ − L₁). When a wave of wavelength λ travels an extra distance Δd, its phase advances by 2π × (Δd/λ), so the two daughter waves arriving back at the splitter have a relative phase Δφ. The total electric field at the output is the sum of two harmonic waves of equal amplitude E₀ and a relative phase Δφ:

blocks.caption: Δφ is the phase difference, in radians, accumulated between the two daughter waves over their round trips. λ is the wavelength of the source light in vacuum (or in the medium that fills the arms, if it is not vacuum). L₁ is the optical path length from the beam splitter to mirror M₁. L₂ is the optical path length from the beam splitter to mirror M₂. The factor of 2 outside the parenthesis is the round trip: light travels each arm twice, once outbound to the mirror and once back. The factor 2π/λ is the spatial frequency of the wave: a path of one wavelength accumulates one full cycle of phase, 2π radians.

blocks.text: What the detector measures is the time-averaged intensity of the combined wave, which is the squared amplitude of the sum of two equal-amplitude pieces with relative phase Δφ. Add them: E_total = E₀[cos(ωt) + cos(ωt + Δφ)], where ω is the angular frequency of the source. Use the standard identity cos(A) + cos(B) = 2 cos((A−B)/2) cos((A+B)/2) to rewrite the sum as E_total = 2E₀ cos(Δφ/2) cos(ωt + Δφ/2). The square of this, averaged over an optical cycle (the cos² of the ωt+Δφ/2 piece averages to ½), gives the output intensity in terms of the maximum possible intensity I_max = 2E₀²:

blocks.caption: I(Δφ) is the time-averaged intensity at the output, with units of power per unit area. I_max is the intensity that would arrive at the output if the two daughter waves were perfectly in phase (Δφ a multiple of 2π); it is the maximum the detector can register and equals twice the intensity of either single daughter wave on its own. Δφ is the relative phase of the two daughter waves at the recombiner. L₁ and L₂ are the two arm lengths. λ is the source wavelength. The output cycles between zero (perfect destructive interference, the dark fringe) and I_max (perfect constructive, the bright fringe) as L₂ − L₁ sweeps through half a wavelength. One full bright-to-bright cycle is a fringe.

blocks.text: Move M₂ by exactly λ/2 and the path difference 2(L₂ − L₁) changes by one wavelength, the phase changes by 2π, and the output cycles back to whatever brightness it had before. The mirror moves half a wavelength, but the count of fringes counts the change in round-trip distance in units of λ. This is the entire reason the instrument is sensitive: every fringe is one wavelength of round-trip displacement, and a human eye can split a fringe by a factor of 50 to 100. With λ = 589 nm sodium light, one hundredth of a fringe is about 6 nm of path difference, or 3 nm of mirror displacement. With a 1.06 μm Nd:YAG laser (the source LIGO now uses), the equivalent in modern detectors is improved by another nineteen orders of magnitude, of which most comes from squeezing every part of the apparatus.

blocks.caption: An interactive Michelson interferometer. The source on the left emits a wave that the beam splitter divides into two arms, one going right to mirror M₁, one going up to mirror M₂. Each daughter wave reflects, returns to the splitter, and recombines toward the detector at the bottom. Drag the slider to move M₂ in nanometers; the path-difference panel shows 2(L₂ − L₁), the phase wheel shows Δφ in units of 2π, and the detector face shows the output intensity I/I_max = cos²(Δφ/2). Switch the source between sodium (589 nm) and Nd:YAG (1064 nm) to see why the same fringe shift corresponds to a different physical displacement for each wavelength.

quiz.question: A Michelson interferometer is illuminated by helium-neon laser light at λ = 633 nm. While you slowly translate one of the arm mirrors, you watch the central fringe at the detector and count exactly 42 bright-to-bright cycles before the mirror stops moving. By how much did the mirror move, and by how much did the round-trip path difference change?
options: The mirror moved 42 × 633 nm ≈ 26.6 μm, and the round-trip path difference changed by the same amount. One bright-to-bright cycle is one wavelength of mirror travel.

The mirror moved 42 × 633 / 2 nm ≈ 13.3 μm, and the round-trip path difference changed by 42 × 633 nm ≈ 26.6 μm. One bright-to-bright cycle is one full wavelength of round-trip path difference, but because the light traverses each arm twice (outbound and return), the mirror only moves half a wavelength per fringe.
The mirror moved 42 × 633 / 4 nm ≈ 6.6 μm. The factor of 4 is the round-trip plus the splitter delay.
It is impossible to say without knowing which arm the mirror was in and what the other arm length was.
quiz.explanation: Option 0 forgets the round trip: every photon detected has bounced off the mirror once, so when the mirror moves by Δx the path length to that mirror changes by Δx but the round-trip optical distance changes by 2Δx, and the path difference between the two arms changes by 2Δx as well. Option 2 invents a phantom factor of 4 that has no source in the geometry. Option 3 mistakes the calibration for an absolute measurement; the count of fringes measures the change in L₂ − L₁ regardless of either arm's absolute length, which is the whole reason the interferometer is useful as a comparator. The honest answer is option 1. The intensity formula I = I_max cos²[2π(L₂ − L₁)/λ] cycles bright-to-bright every time L₂ − L₁ changes by λ. If only one mirror moves, by Δx, then L₂ − L₁ changes by Δx, and the number of fringes is N = Δx/λ. Wait: that is mirror displacement per fringe = λ. So 42 fringes means the mirror moved 42λ? Let me recount. The intensity is cos²(2π(L₂ − L₁)/λ), so the argument advances by π (not 2π) for one bright-to-dark-to-bright cycle, because cos² has period π in its argument. So 2π(L₂ − L₁)/λ must advance by π per fringe, which means (L₂ − L₁) advances by λ/2 per fringe, which means the mirror moves λ/2 per fringe. Forty-two fringes therefore is 42 × (633/2) nm ≈ 13.3 μm of mirror motion. The round-trip path difference is twice that, 26.6 μm. Option 1 is correct: the mirror moves a half wavelength per fringe because cos² has period π in its argument, and the round-trip difference moves a full wavelength because it doubles the mirror displacement. The two pieces of the answer are not the same number, and that mismatch is exactly where most introductory Michelson problems go wrong.

sections.heading: 3. 1887: the null result, and what theory had to do about it

blocks.text: Michelson's 1881 instrument in Potsdam was too small and too vibration-prone to give a clean answer, and the result he published in the American Journal of Science was inconclusive. He returned to Cleveland and teamed with the chemist Edward Morley at what is now Case Western Reserve University. In 1887 the two of them built the apparatus the textbooks now call the Michelson-Morley experiment: a sandstone slab 1.5 meters on a side, floating in a pool of mercury so that it could be rotated by hand to any compass direction with negligible mechanical stress, and carrying mirrors at the end of each of two perpendicular arms eleven meters long when folded by additional fold mirrors. They observed in summer (when Earth's orbital velocity vector pointed one way through space) and in fall (when it pointed differently), at noon and at evening, and they reduced the data to a single number: the expected fringe shift between the two orientations, on the ether-wind hypothesis, would have been about 0.40 fringes. The shift they actually saw was below 0.02 fringes, twenty times smaller than the prediction, consistent with zero.

The community did not absorb the result quickly. George FitzGerald, in Dublin in 1889, suggested that the arm pointed along the direction of motion through the ether might be physically contracted in length by exactly the factor √(1 − v²/c²), so that the predicted fringe shift would be exactly cancelled. He left no derivation: it was a guess made to save the appearances. Hendrik Lorentz, in Leiden in 1892 and 1895, derived the same contraction from a microscopic model in which the molecular forces holding solid bodies together were themselves electromagnetic, and would therefore deform in motion through the ether in precisely the way the Maxwell equations of the ether required. Both men kept the ether; both took the null result as evidence that motion through the ether had measurable physical effects on rulers and clocks, but that those effects conspired to make the motion undetectable by any electromagnetic experiment. By the late 1890s the Lorentz transformations (the coordinate change that left Maxwell's equations invariant between two frames in relative motion) were written down in essentially their modern form, by Lorentz and by Henri Poincaré, both still committed to the ether.

Einstein published "Zur Elektrodynamik bewegter Körper" ("On the Electrodynamics of Moving Bodies") in Annalen der Physik in September 1905. The paper does not cite Michelson and Morley by name. In conversation late in life Einstein gave different and incompatible accounts of how much the experimental result had weighed on him during the 1903-1905 period, and historians of physics still disagree. What is clear is that the special theory of relativity took as its starting axiom precisely the negative content of the 1887 experiment: there is no preferred frame, no ether, no absolute velocity. Lorentz's contraction and time dilation, originally invented to save the ether, became kinematic consequences of the new axiom and not separate dynamical assumptions. The instrument had not measured the ether; it had measured its absence, and the absence had a structure that took an entirely new theoretical framework to organize. This is the cleanest case in nineteenth-century physics of a null result with morally positive content.

sections.heading: 4. From ether to gravitational waves: same instrument, different problem

blocks.text: After 1905 the Michelson interferometer settled into routine work as the world's most precise length comparator. By 1893 Michelson himself had used a successor instrument at the International Bureau of Weights and Measures in Sèvres to express the standard meter as a multiple of the wavelength of the red cadmium line, 1,553,164.13 wavelengths to the meter, a definition that survived in international metrology until the 1960 redefinition in terms of the krypton-86 line. The instrument also became the basic optical layout of Fourier-transform infrared spectroscopy (an FTIR spectrometer is a scanning-mirror Michelson with the interferogram Fourier-transformed to yield a spectrum), of laser unequal-arm displacement sensors, and (decisively) of the worldwide network of gravitational wave detectors that became operational between 2002 and 2015.

A passing gravitational wave (a propagating ripple in the metric of spacetime predicted by Einstein in 1916 from the linearized field equations of general relativity) strains spacetime perpendicular to its direction of travel, stretching it along one transverse axis and compressing it along the other in the same instant. If two freely-falling test masses sit at distance L apart along one axis, and a wave of strain amplitude h passes through, their proper separation changes by ΔL = (h/2) L during the half-cycle of stretching. Two pairs of test masses arranged at right angles, one along each transverse axis, see opposite-sign changes in their separations. That is precisely the geometry a Michelson interferometer is built to read: two arms at right angles, comparing their lengths to one part in (Δφ/2π)(λ/2L). The strain h is the natural output of a Michelson reading two free-mass arms:

blocks.caption: h is the dimensionless gravitational-wave strain, the fractional length change of a free-mass pair in the wave's transverse plane. ΔL is the physical length change of one arm. L is the arm length of one arm of the interferometer (the LIGO arms are L = 4 km). λ is the wavelength of the laser used to read the arms (LIGO uses 1064 nm Nd:YAG light). Δφ is the phase difference at the recombiner caused by the strain, measured by the photodiode at the dark-port output. The factor of 2π and the factor of 2 in the denominator convert Δφ (radians) into a fractional length change, using the same Michelson calibration as section 2 with the round-trip factor folded in.

blocks.text: The astrophysical numbers are brutal. A binary black hole inspiral in the local universe (the closest event yet observed, GW150914, was 410 megaparsecs away, about 1.3 billion light-years) produces a peak strain at Earth of h ≈ 10⁻²¹. With L = 4 km, the arm length change ΔL = hL is 4 × 10⁻¹⁸ meters, one one-thousandth the diameter of a single proton. With λ = 1064 nm, the corresponding phase shift on a single pass is Δφ = (2π/λ) × 2ΔL ≈ 5 × 10⁻¹¹ radians. No single-pass Michelson on Earth has the photon shot noise budget to resolve a phase that small in a 30-millisecond integration time. The trick the modern detectors use is to fold the arms back on themselves: each arm of LIGO is itself a Fabry-Perot cavity (a pair of partially-reflecting mirrors at the two ends of the arm, between which the light bounces back and forth roughly 280 times before leaking out toward the splitter), which multiplies the effective arm length by the cavity finesse and brings the phase shift up by a factor of order 300, into a range a stabilized 200-watt laser can detect in the relevant frequency band. The full optical layout also recycles the power that would otherwise leave through the bright port back into the input, gaining another factor of 40.

Every other piece of LIGO is just a way to bring the noise floor down to where a phase shift of 10⁻¹¹ radians becomes visible. The mirrors hang from four-stage pendulum suspensions to filter ground motion above 1 Hz; the test masses are 40 kg of fused silica polished to atomic flatness, with mirror coatings designed to minimize thermal noise; the laser is wavelength-stabilized to 10⁻¹⁹ relative frequency stability against a reference cavity; the entire 4 km vacuum tube of each arm is evacuated to 10⁻⁹ torr to kill stray refractive index changes from residual gas. The detector floor of about h = 10⁻²³ /√Hz in the 100 Hz band represents about thirty years of incremental engineering, from the 1972 Forward prototype at Hughes through the 1980s Garching 30-meter prototype and the 1994 LIGO 40-meter caltech instrument to the 2002 Initial LIGO and the 2015 Advanced LIGO. The instrument that began with one researcher in Potsdam looking for an ether wind became a thousand-person, two-decade engineering project to fold the same geometry into a strain meter at the threshold of what photon counting can resolve.

quiz.question: LIGO has arm length L = 4 km, reads with a Nd:YAG laser at λ = 1064 nm, and each arm is folded into a Fabry-Perot cavity that increases the effective light-storage time by a factor of about 280. The peak strain from GW150914 at Earth was h ≈ 10⁻²¹. By approximately how many radians did the recombined output phase swing at the moment of the merger, before and after the Fabry-Perot cavity enhancement, and which of those numbers must the detector actually resolve?
options: The detector must resolve the peak Δφ on a single pass, about 5 × 10⁻¹¹ radians; the Fabry-Perot cavity does not help with phase but only with stability.
The detector must resolve about 10⁻⁵ radians (the full power-recycled gain × 280 × 40 × single-pass), which corresponds to about a milli-fringe on the photodiode.

The detector must resolve about 1.4 × 10⁻⁸ radians, the single-pass Michelson phase of about 5 × 10⁻¹¹ radians times the cavity round-trip enhancement of about 280. The unenhanced single-pass phase 5 × 10⁻¹¹ radians is too small to read against the laser shot noise in a 30 ms integration. Folding the arm back on itself 280 times multiplies the optical path the strain acts on by 280, multiplying Δφ by the same factor, and bringing the phase shift up to where a stabilized 200 W laser plus power-recycling has enough photon flux to read it.
The detector must resolve a phase shift much greater than 1 radian (because the strain modulates the carrier across a full fringe), which is why the photodiode sees a chirp at audio frequencies.
quiz.explanation: Option 0 misunderstands the Fabry-Perot arm cavity: the cavity is not just a stability device, it physically multiplies the path the strain modulates by the number of round trips before the light leaves, which multiplies Δφ by the same factor. Option 1 overcounts the gain by including power-recycling in the phase budget; power-recycling increases the photon flux on the photodiode and so reduces the shot-noise floor, but it does not change the phase shift produced by a given strain. Option 3 mistakes the chirp signal for a large-amplitude modulation; the actual peak phase shift is microradians at best and the chirp is audio-frequency only because that is the binary inspiral frequency, not because the phase swings by a full fringe. The honest answer is option 2. Compute the single-pass Michelson phase from the formula in the section: ΔL = hL = 10⁻²¹ × 4 × 10³ m = 4 × 10⁻¹⁸ m, and the single-pass round-trip Δφ = (2π/λ) × 2ΔL = (2π / 1.064 × 10⁻⁶) × 8 × 10⁻¹⁸ ≈ 4.7 × 10⁻¹¹ radians. Multiply by the cavity round-trip number 280 to get the effective phase the photodiode reads: 280 × 4.7 × 10⁻¹¹ ≈ 1.3 × 10⁻⁸ radians, or about 13 nanoradians. That is what the detector resolves. Power-recycling does not enter Δφ; it boosts the photon flux from 200 W to about 800 kW circulating in the arm cavities, which lowers the shot-noise contribution to the phase measurement enough to make a 10⁻⁸ radian signal visible against noise in the 100 Hz band. The Fabry-Perot enhancement is the geometric trick that puts the strain signal into a readable range, and shot-noise reduction by power-recycling is the sensitivity trick that lets it be read.

blocks.text: On 14 September 2015 at 09:50:45 UTC, with the two Advanced LIGO detectors at Hanford, Washington and Livingston, Louisiana running their first engineering observation run after a five-year shutdown for the upgrade, a strain signal of peak amplitude h = 1.0 × 10⁻²¹ swept through both detectors. The Livingston instrument saw it first; the Hanford instrument registered the same waveform 7 milliseconds later, the light-travel-time delay between the two sites for a wave coming from the southern celestial hemisphere. The signal lasted 0.2 seconds, sweeping in frequency from 35 Hz to 250 Hz with the characteristic chirp of two compact masses in a tightening orbit. At peak it shook each 4 km arm of each detector by ΔL = hL = 4 × 10⁻¹⁸ meters, a length about one ten-thousandth the diameter of a single proton, and the recombined output at the dark port swung by Δφ ≈ 1.3 × 10⁻⁸ radians.

The waveform was matched against a numerical-relativity template bank of binary black hole inspirals (full general-relativistic simulations of the metric near a coalescing binary, performed in advance on supercomputers and parameterized by the two masses, the spins, and the inclination of the orbit). The best fit, published as GW150914 in Physical Review Letters in February 2016, had a primary mass of 36 M_☉, a secondary mass of 29 M_☉, and a final remnant mass of 62 M_☉. The arithmetic does not balance: the sum of the two initial masses exceeds the remnant by 3 M_☉. Those three solar masses (about 5 × 10³⁰ kilograms, slightly more than the mass of the planet Neptune) had been converted into gravitational waves in roughly the last 0.2 seconds before the merger, radiating at a peak luminosity of about 3.6 × 10⁴⁹ watts. For that fifth of a second, GW150914 outshone every electromagnetic source in the observable universe combined by a factor of roughly fifty, none of it visible to any telescope.

Two things about this number are worth holding still. The first is that Michelson's instrument, in essentially the optical geometry of section 2, with the addition of the Fabry-Perot arm cavities and the power-recycling mirror, was the readout. Drag the same beam splitter and the same two-arm geometry into a vacuum tube 4 km long, hang the mirrors from a quadruple pendulum, recycle the laser, and the apparatus that failed to find the ether in 1887 succeeds in 2015 at reading the redistribution of mass-energy by a black-hole merger 1.3 billion light-years away. The second is what was implied. The 1887 experiment had ruled out the ether by failing to find it; the 2015 detection ruled in gravitational waves by finding them at exactly the strain Einstein's 1916 linearized field equations predicted, ninety-nine years after the prediction. The first instrument's null result had forced a new theory; the same instrument's positive result, a century later, confirmed a different new theory. The geometry of beam-splitter-plus-two-arms had now sat in the middle of two of the largest theoretical reorganizations of twentieth-century physics, and what was actually built each time was four mirrors, a glass plate, and a way to count fringes.

finalQuiz.question: Two physicists are discussing the Michelson interferometer. The first claims the instrument's role in nineteenth-century physics (the null result of 1887, the path to special relativity) is incidental, and what the geometry really is, is a length comparator that happens to be good enough to detect gravitational waves. The second claims the gravitational-wave detection is incidental, and what the geometry really is, is a test of frame-dependence in light propagation. Which physicist has the better case, and what is the underlying physics that makes the question coherent?
options: Neither has the better case, because the two uses of the instrument are different physics with no connection beyond superficial geometry; the 1887 instrument measured ether wind, the 2015 instrument measures spacetime strain.

Both physicists are missing the same point. The geometry of the Michelson interferometer is, in every case, a phase comparison between two coherent daughter waves whose round-trip optical paths differ by Δd = 2(L₂ − L₁). What changes the path length to be measured is what the apparatus is sensitive to, and this is genuinely the same physics across both eras: in 1887 the supposed effect was an anisotropy in the round-trip time of light in two perpendicular arms due to motion through a medium, with predicted fractional path difference of order (v/c)² ≈ 10⁻⁸; in 2015 the effect is a real anisotropy in the proper round-trip distance of two perpendicular arms due to a transverse-traceless gravitational wave, with fractional length change h ≈ 10⁻²¹. In both cases the measurand is exactly an asymmetric L₂ − L₁ produced by a property of spacetime itself. The 1887 null result told us spacetime has no preferred frame for light propagation; the 2015 positive result told us spacetime has a wave equation for its own geometry. Both are read off the same Michelson Δφ formula. The continuity is not coincidence; the same instrument can read both because both are effects of spacetime structure on light, and the difference is just whether spacetime is doing the trick by being everywhere uniform (1887) or being locally curved by a passing wave (2015).

The first physicist is right. The role of the 1887 experiment in twentieth-century theory was a historical accident, and special relativity would have arrived from Maxwell's equations regardless. What matters about the Michelson geometry is its precision as a length comparator.

The second physicist is right. The 2015 detection was a special case of the 1887 logic: the Earth was moving through a gravitational wave instead of an ether wind, and the instrument was sensitive to motion through the wave by exactly the same path-difference formula.
finalQuiz.explanation: Option 0 misses the structural connection: in both eras the instrument measures L₂ − L₁, and what is being asked is whether the geometry of spacetime itself produces such a difference. Option 2 dismisses the historical role of the null result too cheaply; Einstein's path to the 1905 paper went through Lorentz, FitzGerald, and the puzzle of why the ether kept hiding, and the Michelson-Morley result was a key piece of why the puzzle could not be dropped. Option 3 oversimplifies the relativistic picture in the other direction; a gravitational wave is not a co-moving ether wind, it is a transverse-traceless perturbation of the metric with no preferred rest frame, and the geometry the Michelson reads in 2015 cannot be reduced to a 1887 ether-wind setup. The honest answer is option 1. The Michelson interferometer is, in every era, a phase comparator: Δφ = (4π/λ)(L₂ − L₁). What changes is what spacetime does to L₂ − L₁. In 1887 the predicted effect was an asymmetry in the round-trip time of light along two orthogonal directions in a supposed ether at rest in some absolute frame; the null result said there is no such ether, no such absolute frame, and special relativity was the theory that turned the null into a principle. In 2015 the predicted (and detected) effect was a real transverse strain h ≈ 10⁻²¹ from a binary black hole merger 1.3 billion light-years away, a propagating wave in the metric of spacetime predicted by linearizing the Einstein field equations of general relativity. Both effects act on Δφ through the same formula; both are properties of spacetime structure, not properties of light or of any medium light moves in. The instrument's continuity is the instrument's depth. Michelson built a phase comparator, and what physics has done with it twice over has been to read off two of spacetime's most consequential geometric facts: that it has no preferred frame, and that it carries waves.
